items.correct: Output per unit of input (per worker, per hour) — measuring efficiency, not total output. A small efficient firm can be more productive than a large inefficient one.
items.misconception: Productivity is the total amount produced by a business.
items.random_wrong: Productivity is the number of employees a business has.
items.why: Total output and productivity are different. A factory of 100 workers producing 1000 units is less productive than 10 workers producing 200 units. The ratio matters, not the total.

items.q: What is Herzberg's distinction between hygiene factors and motivators?
items.correct: Hygiene factors (pay, conditions, policies) prevent dissatisfaction when present but don't motivate; motivators (recognition, achievement, responsibility) create positive satisfaction.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness.
items.why: Pay is a hygiene factor — its absence demotivates but its presence doesn't motivate beyond a point. Students treat pay as the prime motivator; Herzberg's theory directly challenges this.

items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: Correct answer: Specific. “Strategic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Friedman. “Drucker” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: The real figure is 5 elements. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
